license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.21203/rs.3.pex-1170/v1
title: A protocol for the extraction, identification, and quantification of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) in silages using Reverse Phase – High Performance Liquid Chromatography with Diode Array Detector (RP-HPLC-DAD)
author: Julián ndrés Castillo Vargas

steps_raw.instruction: On the day of the analysis, thaw the fried samples at room temperature (20 ºC).
Weigh 10 g fresh silage and transfer to glass jar blender.
Add 100 mL of Milli-Q water (measured using a class A volumetric pipette) to the glass jar blender.
Blend the mixture during 30s at an intermediate rate.
Filtrate the resulting mixture using a PYREX™ Gooch Type Filtering Crucible and vacuum filtration equipment. Keep the liquid filtrate.
Transfer 2 mL of the filtrate to 6 mL plastic centrifugation tubes and add 2 mL of extraction solution (see 3.1 section).
Vortex the mixture during 30s at an intermediate rate.
Centrifugate the mixture at 10,000 x G for 15 min at 4 ºC.

Recover a portion of the liquid phase after centrifugation using a plastic syringe and filter it through a 0.45 μm PES membrane filter, collecting a minimum of 500 uL of the filtrate in a chromatographic vial.

5.2.1 Chromatographic analysis of the calibration curves (lactic acid and SCFAs): Prepare the calibration curves for lactic acid and SCFAs as described in materials sections 3.3.1 and 3.3.2, respectively (reagents).
Analyze each point of the calibration curve using RP-HPLC-DAD.
Identify and integrate the resulting chromatographic peaks.
5.2.2. Chromatographic analysis of silage extract samples: Place 500 μL of the final extract in a chromatographic vial.
Analyze using RP-HPLC-DAD.
Identify and integrate the resulting chromatographic peaks.

5.2.3. Chromatographic conditions: - Column temperature: 30 ºC. - Injection volume: 20 μL. - Stationary phase: Hypersil GOLD C18 column (dimensions: 150 x 4.6 mm) equipped with Hypersil GOLD C18 guard column (dimensions: 10 x 4 mm). - Working wavelength: 210 nm. - Mode: gradient, constant flow: 0.750 mL/min (Figure 3). Note: According to the literature findings, the most suitable column for SCFAs chromatographic separation by HPLC-DAD is an ion-exchange column. However, the Hypersil GOLD C18 column allows an efficient SCFAs separation, except for butyric and iso-butyric acids. This condition may constitute a limitation of the Hypersil GOLD C18 column use for SCFAs determination. However, considering that the concentration of iso-butyric acid is significantly lower in silages, a good estimation of butyric acid can be obtained with the reported method herein.

1. According to Hypersil GOLD C18 column manufacturer, the secure pH range of buffer use is between 2 – 8. Therefore, in case of future optimizations of this chromatographic method, the use of solvents or mobile phases with a pH out of this range must be avoided to preserve the activity of the stationary phase. 2. The column used in the proposed method does not allow the use of 100% aqueous mobile phases (example: the use of solutions of inorganic acids). Therefore, in the case of future optimizations of this method, the use of a mobile phase 100% aqueous must be avoided.

- Mobile phase Reagent preparations: 10 min (for 50 samples). - Sample preparation: 30 min (for one sample). - Calibration curve preparation: 242 min for developing the calibration curves (for 50 samples) - HPLC-DAD set-up and stabilization: 15 min (for 50 samples). - HPLC-DAD sample run time: 22 min (for one sample). - Data analysis: 5 min (for one sample).

The successful identification of lactic and acetic acids, as well as propionic acid (main fatty acids in common silage samples), are presented in Figure 4. Calibration curves for the SCFAs commonly found in different silage samples around the world are presented in Figure 5. It is important to point out that this protocol was tested using corn silage samples with satisfactory results.
